intro.kicker: Topic 14 of 38 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.description: A mass m₂ hangs over a frictionless pulley and pulls a mass m₁ resting on a rough horizontal or inclined surface. The minimum m₂ needed to just start the motion of m₁ is found at the limiting friction condition. For horizontal surface: m₂ = μm₁. For inclined surface (angle θ): m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ). NEET tests both variants with numerical substitution and conceptual MCQs.

expectedQuestions.description: Appears once per 2–3 years in NEET in the form: ‘find minimum m₂ to start motion of m₁’. Both horizontal and inclined plane variants are tested. The horizontal case is higher frequency. The inclined variant requires correct sign: m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) when m₁ tends to slide down and m₂ tension just overcomes this. Always draw the FBD clearly before applying the limiting condition.

timeRequired.description: 20 min on FBD analysis and deriving both formulas, 15 min on MCQ practice. Key insight: the string tension T equals m₂g at the moment of just starting motion (Atwood setup with pulley). For m₁ on horizontal rough surface: T = μm₁g ⇒ m₂g = μm₁g ⇒ m₂ = μm₁. For inclined: T = m₁g(sinθ + μ cosθ) (when m₁ is pulled up the incline by m₂).

difficulty.description: Moderate difficulty: requires FBD skill, correct friction direction, and applying limiting condition T = Fl. Common NEET error: using T = m₂g + m₁g instead of treating m₁ and m₂ as separate systems. Also tricky: the direction of friction on m₁ depends on whether m₂ tends to pull m₁ up-slope or m₁ slides down. Read the problem statement carefully.

nriUsaGap.description: AP Physics covers Atwood machine and inclined plane separately; the connected two-mass friction pulley problem is in NEET scope. US students should be comfortable with the FBD approach. Practise: (1) horizontal: T = μm₁g; (2) inclined pulling up slope: T = m₁g(sinθ + μ cosθ). Note that if m₁ tends to slide down without m₂ you would set up T + Fl = m₁g sinθ, which changes the formula.

weightage.sectionTitle: Minimum Mass Hung from String — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Horizontal surface formula m₂ = μm₁ is the most common variant.
Inclined plane variant adds the sinθ term: m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ).
Coefficient of friction given; compute minimum m₂ — straightforward substitution.

strategySteps.title: Draw FBD for Each Mass Separately
strategySteps.description: Mass m₂ (hanging): T = m₂g at limiting equilibrium. Mass m₁ (on surface): T = friction force at limiting condition. For horizontal: T = μm₁g ⇒ m₂g = μm₁g. For inclined (m₁ pulled up slope): T = m₁g(sinθ + μ cosθ). Always equalise the tension T.

strategySteps.title: Identify Friction Direction
strategySteps.description: Friction opposes the tendency of motion of m₁. When m₂ pulls m₁ via string: friction on m₁ acts away from m₂ direction. On incline where m₁ is pulled up: friction acts down-slope (opposing up-slope tendency), so both gravity (sinθ) and friction (μ cosθ) resist the pull. Hence m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ).

strategySteps.title: Check If Problem Asks for Minimum or Maximum m₂
strategySteps.description: Minimum m₂ to START motion = limiting friction condition. If the question asks for maximum m₂ that still keeps m₁ stationary (e.g. m₁ on incline tending to slide DOWN), friction acts up-slope and resists gravity down-slope, so T + Fl = m₁g sinθ and m₂ = m₁(sinθ − μ cosθ). The sign depends on problem geometry.

cards.title: Minimum Mass Hung from String Notes
cards.description: Full derivation of m₂ = μm₁ (horizontal) and m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) (inclined). Includes FBDs, sign convention, 6 worked NEET examples, and all four sub-cases of inclined geometry.

cards.title: Pulley-Friction Quick Reference Card
cards.description: One-page summary: FBD diagrams for both horizontal and inclined cases, formula box with sign rules, and extreme-case verification table.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Minimum Mass Hung from String

cards.title: Horizontal Surface Case
cards.coreRevision: Setup: mass m₁ on rough horizontal surface connected via string over frictionless pulley to hanging mass m₂. Limiting friction on m₁ = μm₁g. At the moment of starting motion, tension T = m₂g equals limiting friction: m₂g = μm₁g. Therefore minimum m₂ = μm₁.
points: Condition: T = m₂g = μm₁g at start of motion
Pulley is frictionless: tension same throughout string
Normal reaction N = m₁g (horizontal surface, no vertical pull)
cards.example: m₁ = 3 kg, μ = 0.4. Minimum m₂ = 0.4 × 3 = 1.2 kg. If m₂ = 1.2 kg is hung, the system is just about to move. Any m₂ > 1.2 kg starts motion.

cards.title: Inclined Surface Case
cards.coreRevision: Mass m₁ on rough inclined plane (angle θ) connected via string over frictionless pulley at top to hanging mass m₂. String pulls m₁ up the slope. At limiting condition: T = m₁g sinθ + μm₁g cosθ (both gravity component and friction oppose upward motion of m₁). With T = m₂g: minimum m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ).
points: Minimum m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) (inclined plane, m₁ pulled up slope)
Gravity component down slope: m₁g sinθ
Limiting friction (down slope, opposing upward tendency): μm₁g cosθ
Normal reaction N = m₁g cosθ (perpendicular to incline)
At θ = 0° (horizontal): formula reduces to m₂ = μm₁ ✓

cards.title: Limiting Condition and Coefficient of Friction
cards.coreRevision: From the horizontal case at limiting condition: μ = m₂/m₁. This provides a method to measure the coefficient of friction experimentally by determining the minimum hanging mass m₂ that just starts m₁. The ratio m₂/m₁ at just-start-of-motion = μ. This is a commonly tested conceptual point in NEET MCQs.
points: μ = m₂/m₁ (from horizontal case at limiting condition)
Experimental method: find smallest m₂ that starts motion; μ = m₂/m₁
Always use limiting (static) friction for ‘just starts motion’ condition
Use kinetic friction only if asked about motion after it has started
cards.example: Experiment: m₁ = 2 kg on horizontal rough surface. Minimum hanging mass to start motion = 0.6 kg. μ = 0.6/2 = 0.3. The surface has coefficient of static friction = 0.3.

curriculum.hintText: AP Physics and US curricula vs NEET scope on pulley-friction two-mass problems.

gaps.title: AP Physics Covers Atwood but Not Friction-Pulley Combined
gaps.description: AP Physics 1 covers Atwood machines and inclined planes with friction separately, but less emphasis on the combined ‘minimum mass to start motion’ setup. NEET tests direct numerical application: given m₁, μ, and θ, calculate minimum m₂. Practise the formula-application sequence: (1) FBD → (2) T = m₂g → (3) T = Fl at limiting.
bullets: Horizontal: m₂ = μm₁ (very quick once limiting condition is set).
Inclined: m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) — both weight component and friction.

gaps.title: Sign Convention for Inclined Case
gaps.description: The sign in the inclined formula (sinθ ± μ cosθ) depends on the geometry. If m₂ pulls m₁ UP the incline (typical NEET setup): m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ). If m₁ slides DOWN the incline and m₂ opposes: m₂ = m₁(sinθ − μ cosθ). Read each problem carefully for the direction of impending motion of m₁.
bullets: m₁ pulled up slope: both mg sinθ and friction μmg cosθ oppose → + sign.
m₁ tends to slide down: friction is up-slope, assists the string → − sign.

questions.question: A block of mass 5 kg rests on a rough horizontal surface. It is connected by a string over a frictionless pulley to a hanging block of mass m. The coefficient of static friction is 0.4. The minimum value of m to just start the motion is: (NEET-type)
questions.explanation: Minimum m = μm₁ = 0.4 × 5 = 2 kg. At this value, tension T = mg = 0.4×5×9.8 = μm₁g exactly equals limiting friction. The system just begins to move when m = 2 kg.

questions.question: Mass m₁ = 4 kg is placed on a rough inclined plane (angle 30°, μ = 0.3). A mass m₂ is hung via string over frictionless pulley to pull m₁ up the incline. Minimum m₂ is: (sin30°=0.5, cos30°=0.866)
A 10 kg block on a horizontal surface (μ = 0.5) is connected to a hanging mass. Minimum hanging mass to start motion is:
On a rough inclined plane at 45° with μ = 0.2, a block of mass m₁ is connected to hanging mass m₂. Minimum m₂/m₁ ratio to pull m₁ up the slope is approximately:
If coefficient of friction doubles, minimum hanging mass m₂ to start motion on a horizontal surface:
For a horizontal surface, at the moment of just starting motion, the tension in the string equals:

items.title: Derive m₂ = μm₁ for horizontal surface
items.description: FBD of m₂: T = m₂g. FBD of m₁: T = μm₁g at limiting. Equate: m₂g = μm₁g ⇒ m₂ = μm₁.

items.title: Derive m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) for inclined surface
items.description: m₁ pulled up incline: T = m₁g sinθ + μm₁g cosθ at limiting. T = m₂g ⇒ m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ).

items.title: Verify θ = 0° reduces to horizontal formula
items.description: sin0° = 0, cos0° = 1: m₂ = m₁(0 + μ×1) = μm₁. Inclined formula reduces correctly to horizontal. Good sanity check.

items.title: Know sign change for sliding-down scenario
items.description: If m₁ tends to slide DOWN incline and m₂ just prevents it: friction acts up-slope helping string, so T + Fl = m₁g sinθ → m₂ = m₁(sinθ − μ cosθ). Sign depends on direction of impending motion.

items.question: Why do we set T = m₂g for the hanging mass at limiting condition?
items.answer: At the instant of starting motion (limiting equilibrium), the acceleration is zero. For the hanging mass m₂: net force = m₂g − T = 0 ⇒ T = m₂g. This is equal to the weight of m₂. As the system begins to move, acceleration becomes non-zero, but for the ‘just starting’ (minimum mass) condition, the system is in limiting equilibrium with zero acceleration.

items.question: Is the normal reaction on m₁ equal to m₁g in both horizontal and inclined cases?
items.answer: For horizontal surface: N = m₁g (weight balanced directly by normal reaction). For inclined surface: N = m₁g cosθ (only the perpendicular component of weight is balanced by normal reaction). This is why the friction on inclined surface is μm₁g cosθ, not μm₁g.

items.question: What happens if m₂ is larger than the minimum value?
items.answer: If m₂ exceeds the minimum value, the net force on the system becomes non-zero and the system accelerates. In this case, kinetic friction acts on m₁ (not static limiting friction). The acceleration is calculated using T − μm₁g = (m₁+m₂)a — this is a different calculation from the minimum condition. NEET usually tests specifically the ‘just starts’ condition using limiting (static) friction.

items.question: If the pulley is not frictionless, how does the calculation change?
items.answer: A frictionless pulley ensures the tension T is the same throughout the string (both sides equal T = m₂g). If the pulley has friction, the tensions on the two sides differ: T₂ on the hanging side and T₁ on the m₁ side, with T₁ < T₂ or T₁ > T₂ depending on the rotation direction. NEET problems almost always specify frictionless pulley, so T is equal on both sides.

grounding_summary: Page 242 (textbook p. 229) and page 243 (textbook p. 230) cover the minimum mass hung from string setup for both horizontal and inclined surface. The textbook derives T = m₂g at limiting equilibrium and equates it to limiting friction on m₁ for both configurations.

Page 243 explicitly states the condition for minimum m₂ using ‘this is the minimum value of m₂’ and provides the formula m₂ = m₁(sinθ + μ cosθ) for the inclined case. The string runs over a frictionless pulley.
Page 242 provides context from adjacent two-body friction problems confirming understanding of limiting friction and separate FBD analysis for each mass.
grounding_excerpt: this is the minimum value of
the component of its weight along the bowl is balanced by limiting frictional force

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Friction Applications)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source covering the pulley-mass-friction setup with derivation of minimum hanging mass for both horizontal and inclined surface configurations.

sources.title: NEET Physics Class 11 Chapter 6 Friction Notes — ClassNotes.guru
sources.why_used: NEET-targeted notes confirming minimum mass formulae and both horizontal and inclined plane cases as NEET exam patterns.

review.notes: Content grounded on page-242 and page-243 OCR. Formulas m2=mu*m1 (horizontal) and m2=m1(sin_theta+mu*cos_theta) (inclined) derived from textbook limiting friction conditions. Frictionless pulley assumption confirmed by source.
